Gen.author: Moses (traditional); compiled c. 1446–1406 BC
Gen.date: c. 1446–1406 BC (events span Creation to c. 1805 BC)
Gen.audience: Israel at Sinai and entering Canaan
Gen.theme: Origins — God creates, judges, and chooses a covenant family
Gen.summary: From the seven days of creation to Joseph's death in Egypt, Genesis traces how a holy God establishes humanity, judges sin (the Flood), and selects Abraham's line as the channel through whom every nation will be blessed.

Exod.author: Moses
Exod.theme: Redemption — God delivers His people and dwells among them
Exod.summary: Israel groans under Egyptian bondage. God raises Moses, sends the ten plagues, parts the Red Sea, gives the Ten Commandments at Sinai, and instructs the building of the tabernacle so His glory can dwell with His people.

Lev.author: Moses
Lev.theme: Holiness — how a sinful people approach a holy God
Lev.summary: Sacrificial system, priesthood, dietary laws, festivals, and the Day of Atonement. The repeated refrain — 'Be holy, for I am holy' — frames every regulation as covenantal proximity to God.

Num.author: Moses
Num.theme: Wilderness wandering — faithfulness tested across forty years
Num.summary: Two censuses bracket Israel's failure to enter Canaan. Rebellion, complaint, and the long detour shape a generation. Despite unfaithfulness, God preserves His covenant promises and brings the next generation to the brink of the Promised Land.

Deut.author: Moses
Deut.audience: Israel on the plains of Moab before crossing the Jordan
Deut.theme: Love and obey the LORD with all your heart
Deut.summary: Moses' farewell sermons re-cite the Law for the new generation, structured as an ancient suzerain-vassal covenant. Blessings and curses, the Shema (Deut 6:4–5), and the call to choose life or death define Israel's identity.

Josh.author: Joshua (traditional, with later additions)
Josh.theme: God's faithfulness in fulfilling the land promise
Josh.summary: Crossing the Jordan, the fall of Jericho, the conquest campaigns, and the division of the land among the twelve tribes. Joshua's final charge — 'Choose this day whom ye will serve' — closes the book.

Judg.author: Anonymous (traditionally Samuel)
Judg.date: c. 1050–1000 BC; events span c. 1380–1050 BC
Judg.theme: When everyone did right in his own eyes, the cycle of sin and rescue
Judg.summary: Twelve charismatic deliverers (Othniel, Ehud, Deborah, Gideon, Samson, etc.) rescue Israel from oppressors as the nation cycles through apostasy, distress, repentance, and deliverance. The book closes in moral chaos, anticipating the need for a king.

Ruth.author: Anonymous (traditionally Samuel)
Ruth.date: c. 1010–970 BC; events during the period of the Judges
Ruth.theme: Hesed (loyal love) and the providence of God in Messianic genealogy
Ruth.summary: A Moabite widow's loyalty to her Israelite mother-in-law brings her into the redemptive line of David — and ultimately of Jesus Christ. A quiet pastoral counter-narrative to the violence of Judges.

1Sam.author: Samuel, Nathan, and Gad (with later compilation)
1Sam.date: Compiled c. 930 BC; events c. 1100–1010 BC
1Sam.theme: From judges to kings — the LORD raises up His anointed
1Sam.summary: Samuel transitions Israel from tribal judges to monarchy. Saul is anointed, fails, and is rejected. David, the shepherd-king after God's own heart, is anointed and pursued. Ends with Saul's death at Gilboa.

2Sam.author: Compiled by court historians (Nathan, Gad)
2Sam.theme: David's reign — the rise, the fall, and the eternal covenant
2Sam.summary: David unites Israel, captures Jerusalem, receives the Davidic Covenant (2 Sam 7), but falls into sin with Bathsheba. The consequences ripple through Absalom's revolt. Ends with David's penitent psalm and census.

1Kgs.author: Anonymous (traditionally Jeremiah)
1Kgs.theme: The temple, the divided kingdom, and the prophets who confront kings
1Kgs.summary: Solomon's wisdom, wealth, temple-building, and apostasy. The kingdom splits (Israel vs. Judah). Elijah confronts Ahab and Jezebel on Mount Carmel.

2Kgs.author: Anonymous (traditionally Jeremiah)
2Kgs.theme: Why both kingdoms fell — covenant infidelity and prophetic warning unheeded
2Kgs.summary: Elisha's miracles, the fall of Israel to Assyria (722 BC), Judah's reform under Hezekiah and Josiah, and finally the Babylonian destruction of Jerusalem (586 BC) and exile.

1Chr.author: Ezra (traditional)
1Chr.theme: Davidic covenant and temple worship — Israel's true identity
1Chr.summary: Genealogies from Adam to David, then a re-telling of David's reign focused on temple preparation. Written to remind returnees who they are: the people of David, the people of the temple.

2Chr.author: Ezra (traditional)
2Chr.theme: Judah's kings measured by faithfulness to the temple
2Chr.summary: Solomon's temple, the divided kingdom told from Judah's perspective only, and the eventual exile. Closes with Cyrus's decree allowing return — a hopeful coda.

Ezra.author: Ezra (and earlier sources)
Ezra.theme: Restoration of temple and covenant under Persian rule
Ezra.summary: Two return waves: Zerubbabel rebuilds the temple (c. 538–516 BC), Ezra returns to teach the Law (c. 458 BC) and confront mixed marriages. Persia funds the work — God uses pagan empires.

Neh.author: Nehemiah
Neh.theme: Rebuilding the walls — civic and spiritual restoration
Neh.summary: Nehemiah, cupbearer to Artaxerxes, leads the rebuilding of Jerusalem's walls in 52 days against opposition. With Ezra he calls Israel to covenant renewal.

Esth.author: Anonymous
Esth.theme: God's hidden providence — He rescues His people even when His name is unspoken
Esth.summary: A Jewish girl becomes queen of Persia. Haman plots genocide; Esther risks her life and Mordecai is exalted. The Feast of Purim is established. God's name never appears, yet He is everywhere.

Job.author: Anonymous (perhaps Moses or Solomon)
Job.date: Events possibly patriarchal era; written 2000–500 BC
Job.theme: Why do the righteous suffer? — God is sovereign even in the inscrutable
Job.summary: A blameless man loses everything. Three friends offer wrong answers. Elihu adds a fourth voice. Then God speaks from the whirlwind, not explaining but revealing Himself — and Job worships.

Ps.author: David (73), Asaph, sons of Korah, Solomon, Moses, anonymous
Ps.theme: The full range of human emotion offered to God — praise, lament, repentance, hope
Ps.summary: 150 psalms in five books. Songs for every condition: anguish, joy, repentance, praise, royal coronation, Messianic prophecy. The hymnbook of Israel and the prayer-book of the church.

Prov.author: Solomon (primarily); also Agur, Lemuel
Prov.theme: The fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom
Prov.summary: Practical proverbs on speech, work, sex, money, friendship, and humility. Wisdom is personified as a woman calling out in the streets, set against the seductress of folly.

Eccl.author: 'Qoheleth' / The Preacher (traditionally Solomon)
Eccl.theme: Vanity of vanities — life under the sun is fleeting; fear God and keep His commandments
Eccl.summary: An honest, even bleak, search for meaning in pleasure, work, wisdom, and wealth — all 'hevel' (vapor). Concludes that man's whole duty is to fear God and obey Him, with judgment ahead.

Song.author: Solomon
Song.theme: The beauty and intimacy of marital love — read also as God's love for His people
Song.summary: Eight chapters of dialogue between bride and groom, alternating courtship, longing, consummation, and wedded delight. Read literally as a celebration of marriage; read typologically as Christ and the Church.

Isa.author: Isaiah son of Amoz
Isa.theme: The Holy One of Israel — judgment and the promised Servant
Isa.summary: Sometimes called 'the fifth gospel.' Confronts Judah's idolatry, prophesies the virgin birth (7:14), the Suffering Servant (53), and the new heavens and new earth (65–66). Quoted more in the NT than any prophet.

Jer.author: Jeremiah, with Baruch as scribe
Jer.audience: Judah on the eve of Babylonian exile
Jer.theme: Covenant infidelity, certain judgment, and the New Covenant
Jer.summary: The 'weeping prophet' watches Jerusalem fall. He prophesies seventy years of exile, the New Covenant written on hearts (31:31–34), and personally suffers for his faithful witness.

Lam.author: Jeremiah (traditional)
Lam.theme: Grief over Jerusalem's destruction — yet 'His mercies are new every morning' (3:22–23)
Lam.summary: Five acrostic poems (chs 1, 2, 4 are 22-verse acrostics; ch 3 is a triple acrostic of 66 verses). Total devastation gives way to a sliver of hope that anchors generations of faith.

Ezek.author: Ezekiel son of Buzi (priest and prophet)
Ezek.theme: The glory of God departs — and returns
Ezek.summary: Visions of the four-faced living creatures, the dry bones, and the future temple. The glory leaves the temple before the destruction (chs 8–11), and returns to an eschatological temple (chs 40–48).

Dan.author: Daniel
Dan.theme: God rules over kingdoms — He raises and removes empires
Dan.summary: Daniel's faithfulness in pagan courts (lions' den, fiery furnace) and his apocalyptic visions of four kingdoms, the seventy weeks (9:24–27), and the everlasting kingdom of the Son of Man (7:13–14).

Hos.author: Hosea son of Beeri
Hos.theme: God's covenant love for an unfaithful spouse
Hos.summary: Hosea's marriage to Gomer dramatizes God's relationship with idolatrous Israel. Despite repeated betrayal, divine love pursues her — a stunning picture of grace.

Joel.author: Joel son of Pethuel
Joel.theme: The Day of the LORD — judgment now and outpoured Spirit later
Joel.summary: Locusts as harbinger of the Day of the LORD. Call to repentance. Prophecy of the Spirit poured out on all flesh (2:28–32) — fulfilled at Pentecost (Acts 2).

Amos.author: Amos of Tekoa
Amos.audience: Northern Kingdom at the height of prosperity
Amos.theme: Let justice roll down like waters
Amos.summary: A shepherd from Judah preaches in the wealthy Northern Kingdom. Indicts social injustice, religious hypocrisy, and complacency. 'The day of the LORD is darkness, and not light.'

Obad.author: Obadiah
Obad.theme: Edom's pride brought down — God avenges His covenant brother
Obad.summary: The shortest OT book (21 verses). Edom rejoiced at Jerusalem's fall; God will judge them in turn. 'As thou hast done, it shall be done unto thee.'

Jonah.author: Jonah son of Amittai
Jonah.theme: God's mercy extends even to wicked Nineveh
Jonah.summary: Jonah flees God's call, is swallowed by a great fish, finally preaches to Nineveh, and pouts when they repent. Jesus cites Jonah as a sign of His own death and resurrection (Matt 12:40).

Mic.author: Micah of Moresheth
Mic.theme: What does the LORD require? — to do justice, love mercy, walk humbly (6:8)
Mic.summary: Foretells Jerusalem's destruction, the birth of the Messiah in Bethlehem (5:2), and the future glory of Mount Zion.

Nah.author: Nahum the Elkoshite
Nah.theme: Nineveh's overthrow — God's judgment is sure
Nah.summary: A century after Jonah, Nineveh has reverted to brutality. Nahum's poetic indictment foretells the city's fall to Babylon and Medes (612 BC). The Lord is jealous and just.

Hab.author: Habakkuk
Hab.audience: Judah on the cusp of Babylonian invasion
Hab.theme: How can a holy God use a wicked nation? — 'The just shall live by faith' (2:4)
Hab.summary: A dialogue between the prophet and God. Twice quoted in the NT, 'the just shall live by faith' becomes the Reformation watchword. Closes with one of Scripture's most beautiful confessions of trust.

Zeph.author: Zephaniah
Zeph.theme: The Day of the LORD — judgment then restoration
Zeph.summary: Universal judgment that consumes earth and Judah, but ends with one of the OT's most tender passages: 'The LORD thy God in the midst of thee... will rejoice over thee with joy... will joy over thee with singing' (3:17).

Hag.author: Haggai
Hag.theme: Rebuild the temple — put God first
Hag.summary: Four short messages over four months stir the returnees to resume temple construction. 'Consider your ways' — they had built their own homes while God's house lay in ruins.

Zech.author: Zechariah son of Berechiah
Zech.theme: Messianic hope — the coming King and the day of His glory
Zech.summary: Eight night visions, then prophecies of the Messiah riding on a donkey (9:9), pierced (12:10), the Shepherd struck (13:7), and reigning over all the earth (14).

Mal.author: Malachi
Mal.audience: Post-exilic Judah, ~100 years after the second temple
Mal.theme: Spiritual apathy rebuked — the messenger of the covenant is coming
Mal.summary: Six disputations between God and a complacent Judah. Closes with the promise of Elijah's return (4:5–6) — 400 years of OT silence ensue, broken by John the Baptist.

Matt.author: Matthew (Levi), tax-collector turned apostle
Matt.theme: Jesus is the long-promised Messiah, Son of David, King of the Jews
Matt.summary: Structured around five great teaching blocks (echoing Moses' Pentateuch). Heavy use of OT quotation showing fulfillment. Begins with genealogy from Abraham; ends with the Great Commission.

Mark.author: John Mark (companion of Peter)
Mark.theme: Jesus the suffering Servant — the Son of God in action
Mark.summary: The shortest, fastest-paced Gospel. 'Immediately' appears 41 times. Centered on the Son of Man's mission to give His life as a ransom for many (10:45).

Luke.author: Luke the physician
Luke.theme: Jesus the Savior of all people — especially the marginalized
Luke.summary: The most carefully researched Gospel (1:1–4). Uniquely emphasizes women, the poor, and Gentiles. Includes the parables of the Good Samaritan and Prodigal Son.

John.author: John the apostle, son of Zebedee
John.theme: Jesus is God in flesh — believe and have eternal life
John.summary: Seven 'I AM' statements, seven signs, and the deepest meditation on Christ's deity. Includes the Upper Room Discourse (chs 13–17) and Jesus' high-priestly prayer.

Acts.author: Luke
Acts.theme: From Jerusalem to Rome — the Spirit-empowered spread of the gospel
Acts.summary: Pentecost, the church's birth, persecution, Stephen's martyrdom, Paul's conversion and three missionary journeys, ending with Paul preaching unhindered in Rome.

Rom.author: Paul the apostle
Rom.theme: The gospel of righteousness by faith — for Jew and Gentile alike
Rom.summary: Paul's most systematic letter. Sin, justification, sanctification, predestination, Israel's future, and Christian living. The letter that converted Augustine, Luther, Wesley, and Karl Barth.

1Cor.author: Paul
1Cor.theme: Christ-shaped community — addressing division, immorality, worship, and resurrection
1Cor.summary: Confronts factionalism, sexual immorality, lawsuits, marriage questions, idol-meat, the Lord's Supper, spiritual gifts, and the bodily resurrection (ch 15).

2Cor.author: Paul
2Cor.summary: Paul defends his apostleship against rivals. The most personal of his letters: his sufferings, his thorn in the flesh, and the principle that God's strength is made perfect in weakness (12:9).

Gal.author: Paul
Gal.theme: Justification by faith alone — not by works of the Law
Gal.summary: Paul's white-hot defense of the gospel against Judaizers who wanted Gentile believers circumcised. The freedom letter that ignited the Reformation.

Eph.author: Paul
Eph.audience: Ephesian church (and circular to Asia Minor)
Eph.theme: Our riches in Christ and our walk worthy of them
Eph.summary: Three chapters of theology (our heavenly position) followed by three of application (our earthly walk). Includes the armor of God (ch 6) and the great prayer of 3:14–21.

Phil.author: Paul
Phil.theme: Joy in Christ — through every circumstance
Phil.summary: The most personal and joyful of Paul's letters, written from prison. Contains the Christ-hymn (2:5–11) and 'I can do all things through Christ' (4:13).

Col.author: Paul
Col.theme: Christ's supremacy and sufficiency over every philosophy
Col.summary: Counters a syncretistic heresy by exalting Christ as Creator, Sustainer, Head of the church, and the fullness of deity bodily (1:15–20, 2:9). Christ is enough.

1Thess.author: Paul
1Thess.theme: Living in light of Christ's return
1Thess.summary: One of Paul's earliest letters. Mentions Christ's return in every chapter. Includes the rapture passage (4:13–18) and the Day of the Lord (5:1–11).

2Thess.author: Paul
2Thess.theme: Endurance under persecution; the Day of the Lord has not yet come
2Thess.summary: Corrects the misunderstanding that the Day of the Lord had already happened. The man of lawlessness must be revealed first. Be steadfast and work.

1Tim.author: Paul
1Tim.theme: How one ought to behave in the household of God
1Tim.summary: Instructions on church order, qualifications for elders and deacons, false teaching, prayer, and pastoral life. Practical handbook for ministry.

2Tim.author: Paul
2Tim.theme: Endure to the end — preach the Word
2Tim.summary: Paul's last letter, written facing execution. 'I have fought a good fight, I have finished my course' (4:7). Charges Timothy to preach in season and out, even when culture rejects sound doctrine.

Titus.author: Paul
Titus.theme: Sound doctrine and good works among rough churches
Titus.summary: Brief but dense. Elder qualifications, pastoral ethics, gospel summary in 3:4–7, and the call for Christians to be 'zealous of good works.'

Phlm.author: Paul
Phlm.theme: Reconciliation in Christ — receive him as a brother
Phlm.summary: A short personal letter pleading with Philemon to receive his runaway slave Onesimus back as a Christian brother. The seedling of the gospel's social transformation.

Heb.author: Anonymous (Apollos? Barnabas? Paul? — uncertain)
Heb.date: c. AD 60–69 (before the temple's fall in 70)
Heb.audience: Jewish Christians tempted to revert to Judaism
Heb.theme: Christ's superiority — He is greater than angels, Moses, the priesthood, the sacrifices
Heb.summary: An extended argument that Christ's once-for-all sacrifice supersedes the entire Levitical system. The 'Hall of Faith' (ch 11) and 'Let us run with patience the race set before us' (12:1).

Jas.author: James, half-brother of Jesus, leader of the Jerusalem church
Jas.date: c. AD 45–49 (likely the earliest NT book)
Jas.summary: Practical Christian living. Confronts the rich, exhorts patience under trial, controls the tongue, and insists that genuine faith produces visible obedience.

1Pet.author: Peter the apostle
1Pet.audience: Christians in Asia Minor under persecution
1Pet.theme: Hope and holiness in suffering — you are an elect, royal priesthood
1Pet.summary: Encourages persecuted believers with their living hope, exhorts holy conduct, and exalts the Suffering Servant whose stripes brought our healing (2:24).

2Pet.author: Peter (with stylistic variation)
2Pet.theme: Beware false teachers — the Day of the Lord will come
2Pet.summary: Peter's farewell. Confronts scoffers who deny Christ's return, predicts the dissolving of the heavens, and calls for godly living in light of the new heavens and new earth.

1John.author: John the apostle
1John.theme: Tests of genuine faith — light, love, and orthodoxy
1John.summary: Three recurring tests: doctrinal (right view of Christ), moral (obedience), and relational (love of brothers). 'God is love' (4:8). 'These things have I written... that ye may know that ye have eternal life' (5:13).

2John.author: John the apostle ('the elder')
2John.theme: Walk in truth and love — refuse false teachers
2John.summary: Thirteen verses. Don't extend hospitality to those who deny Christ's incarnation. Truth and love together — never separated.

3John.author: John the apostle
3John.theme: Hospitality to faithful workers vs. the selfishness of Diotrephes
3John.summary: Fourteen verses. Commendation of Gaius for hosting traveling missionaries; rebuke of Diotrephes for refusing them. The shortest book in the NT.

Jude.author: Jude, half-brother of Jesus
Jude.theme: Contend for the faith once delivered to the saints
Jude.summary: A fierce warning against apostate teachers, drawing on OT examples of judgment. Closes with the great doxology: 'Now unto him that is able to keep you from falling...' (vv 24–25).

Rev.author: John the apostle, exiled on Patmos
Rev.audience: Seven churches in Asia Minor under Roman persecution
Rev.theme: Jesus reigns — He is coming again, and He will make all things new
Rev.summary: Visions of the glorified Christ, the seven churches, the Lamb who was slain, the seven seals/trumpets/bowls, the fall of Babylon, the marriage supper, the millennium, the great white throne, and the new heavens and new earth. The Bible's final word: 'Even so, come, Lord Jesus.'
